comments.body: This sounds more opinion-based and hypothetical than factual, but what the heck. Personally I'd lean toward items 2 and 3 on your short list. Enforcing style isn't the job of the language (in most cases, that is... things like whitespace in Python are exceptions), and there could be valid internal reasons for "violating" a convention within a given product/team/company/etc. against which the language shouldn't institute barriers. Ultimately at this point the most pragmatic reason would be that changing it would break existing codebases which would be very bad.
Well, if there's no information available, it's all crystal ball.

So far, the answer seems to be: The creators of each language make a decision on how much style should be imposed onto their users. Which is why some conventions are allowed, despite being considered bad style by most.
question boils down to "why do we have liberty?" and I think that's okay to ask at most once in your life. if you disagree, then you're on the side of liberty.
I'm sorry, I not getting your first paragraph.
That is exactly my point. Because it does not adhere to normal writing standards it is very difficult to read.
You seem to be suggesting that a language forbidding even the most blatant convention violations is a potential slippery slope, that would force a specific style onto their programmers.
@aliteralmind - I was hoping to suggest that a language that applies restrictions that do not affect the functionality of the code is unnecessarily restrictive. See my addition.
Good points. I see it as two sides of the same thing. You, as a language creator, would impose no style at all onto your users, which necessarily allows bad style.

answers.body:
Coding style is like writing style. If you write in a style that is diFFicult TO READ And does not read very well your mINd hAs GReat diFFICULTies actUally understanding what you are reading.

If, however, like in normal reading text - it is laid out in a form that matches well with what your mind expects then it is clear and easy to understand.

On the other hand, if the language actually FORCED you to write everything using EXACTLY the the right syntax then not only would it make coding slow and awkward but it would restrict your expressiveness.

Many years ago I came across a language that allowed you to add strange symbols to variable names. Users were allowed to do thing like:

var a=b = a = b;
var c<d = c > d;
if ( a=b & c<d ) ...

i.e. a=b was a perfectly acceptable variable name, so was c<d. As I am sure you would agree, this led to many mistakes of interpretation. Even if a new language allowed that I would not use it. Coding standards are for consistency and helping the human mind understand, syntax is for helping the computer mind understand.

comments.body: You're elaborating on @OldCurmudgeon's last paragraph, which is the basic concept that the creators of these languages are making a decision about: How much style to impose onto their users.

I mean this one: "On the other hand, if the language actually FORCED you to write everything using EXACTLY the the right syntax then not only would it make coding slow and awkward but it would restrict your expressiveness."

answers.body:
Depending on the language designer's intent some languages are more opinionated than others when it comes to implementation and how the designers think things should be done.

The easiest example I can think of right now is Go, which has unit testing and code formatting built in. The designers are of the opinion that things should be done a certain way and they provide you the tools to do it.

Other languages do nothing of the sort like Scala where the language designers were very unopinionated in their implementation and supply you the tools to accomplish any given task in 10 different ways.

This isn't to say that some languages are built under tyrannical rule and others are extremely loose with their requirements. Its merely a choice made by the language designers which we end up having to live with.

3 is a big one.

It's not a language's job to enforce a coding style.

In fact, I once worked with a business that included a pipeline tool that enforced 'good' coding style. It became a nightmare quickly. Even things that were "assumed universal", weren't. For example, there were functions that were more used as a complex constant than as a normal function, and they were capitalized to help call out the use... but the style-enforcer broke it and made the code less readable. Set back code by a week as everyone had to refactor their code. There were built in methods that followed code-style by the book... but the style-enforcer didn't understand it matched, and broke it (for example, tooMany it insisted must be toOMany because "to" was a word, and much more painful examples where non-standard captialization actually made it more readable), and lets not get into the camelCase vs snake_case wars... I think the company probably lost a solid 250k on that one alone because half the programmers came from different programming backgrounds that insisted one was correct and the other group's was incorrect... that was... very messy.

And that doesn't even approach the mess we suffered when we pit the enclosure styles against each other...

function foo(var a) {b(a);}

vs

function foo (var a) {
    b(a);
}

vs

function foo
(
    var a
)
{
    b
     (
         a
     );
}

After the camel_case vs snakeCase fiasco, we begrudgingly appointed an official company code-style person. Stupid decision, because the only people who are going to volunteer for such a position are those with extremely strong and obtuse opinions on code style. Those with lassaiz faire opinions didn't apply because they were willing to let bygones be bygones. Those with standard coding opinions didn't apply because they assumed whoever in there would agree with them.

So we ended up with an overzealous style-nazi coding into the checker to force the last one enclosure style. That was probably the most angry the the office had ever been in my time working there. Pretty sure two top level devs quit over that one.

So.... 3. Definitely 3. It's not for anybody but the developer to enforce their coding style. Any other path is the path of pain.

However, the pipeline linter tool actually had a command to turn off the code linter for a section. This turned out to be the most common line at the top of code files. At the end of the day, the war ended with everybody agreeing the war was stupid (except for the style-nazi who tried to have a meeting every other day to yell about people deactivating the linter that people never showed up to.

title: If some naming conventions, such as uppercase-method-names, are so frowned upon, why do languages allow them at all?
body:
Is there a practical or historical reasoning behind languages allowing the most egregious naming convention taboos? The two most obvious examples are uppercase function names and lowercase class names, which I often see violated in stackoverflow newbie questions.

There is no style-justification that I know of where you can do these things, so why are they even allowed to compile? At the moment, my theories are

  • It was not such a taboo when the language was built,
  • It would make some important edge cases impossible, or
  • It's not the language's job to enforce good style.

I can find nothing on this topic (some links are below).

There are some conventions, such as underscores beginning variable names, or Hungarian notation (the latter of which I have been personally disabused of in comments) that are not overwhelmingly accepted, but are less divisive.

I'm asking this as a Java programmer, but would also be interested in answers form other language's.

Some links:

  • http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naming_convention_(programming)#Java
  • http://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/java/javaOO/methods.html
  • http://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Java_Programming/History
  • How important are naming conventions for getters in Java?
